text:
PALLIATIVE MEDICINE / ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Hospice Enrollment and Goals of Care — Comfort-Focused Management

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Margaret Choi is a 60-year-old woman with metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), now with confirmed progressive hepatic metastases on second-line therapy, presenting for transition to home hospice and comfort-focused care planning. She is accompanied by her husband and adult daughter.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Choi was first diagnosed approximately 18 months ago with borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma (cT3 N1 M0, Stage III by AJCC 8th edition) after presenting to her primary care physician with mid-epigastric pain radiating to the back, obstructive jaundice, and 12 lbs of unintentional weight loss. EUS-guided fine needle biopsy confirmed moderately differentiated ductal adenocarcinoma with SMAD4 loss. Her CA 19-9 at diagnosis was 1,248 U/mL. Biliary obstruction was relieved with ERCP and placement of a covered self-expanding metal biliary stent. Comprehensive genomic profiling (FoundationOne CDx) identified KRAS G12V, TP53 R248W, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, SMAD4 R361H, ARID1A Q1178*, and STK11 frameshift mutation, along with MYC and GATA6 amplification. The tumor was MSS/pMMR, TMB-low, with no BRCA1/2 or NTRK alterations — a genomic profile without actionable therapeutic targets under currently available approved agents.

She was treated with neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX) for 8 cycles, achieving an excellent partial response with CA 19-9 declining to 85 U/mL and the primary tumor decreasing from 3.8 cm to 2.4 cm with resolution of SMA abutment. This was followed by consolidative long-course chemoradiation (IMRT 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions with concurrent capecitabine), after which the tumor measured 2.2 cm with maintained vascular clearance and a CA 19-9 of 62 U/mL.

She subsequently underwent a pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) with R0 resection. Surgical pathology revealed ypT2 ypN2 M0 disease with a College of American Pathologists tumor regression grade 2 (moderate response) and 4 of 24 lymph nodes positive for metastatic adenocarcinoma — high-risk features portending significant recurrence risk. Her postoperative course was complicated by a Grade B pancreatic fistula and Grade B delayed gastric emptying, requiring an 18-day hospitalization and placement of a Jackson-Pratt drain at discharge. She was initiated on pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (Creon) for exocrine pancreatic insufficiency and a basal-bolus insulin regimen for new post-pancreatectomy diabetes mellitus.

Adjuvant chemotherapy with gemcitabine and capecitabine (GemCap, per the ESPAC-4 protocol) was commenced at 75% of full dose given her marginal performance status and recent prolonged surgical recovery. Midpoint restaging after 3 cycles of GemCap demonstrated two new small hepatic hypodensities in segments VI and VII, subsequently characterized on gadoxetate-enhanced MRI (Eovist) as showing rim enhancement, arterial enhancement with washout, and hepatobiliary phase hypointensity — features highly concerning for metastatic disease. CT-guided core needle biopsy of the segment VI lesion confirmed metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma (CK7+, CK20-, SMAD4 loss confirmed), establishing Stage IV (M1) disease during adjuvant therapy. The CA 19-9 at this juncture had risen to 58 U/mL.

GemCap was discontinued, and the patient was transitioned to second-line palliative chemotherapy with nanoliposomal irinotecan (nal-IRI, Onivyde) at a dose-reduced level (50 mg/m2, further reduced to 35 mg/m2 after cycle 2) plus leucovorin and continuous-infusion 5-fluorouracil, based on the NAPOLI-1 regimen. UGT1A1 genotyping prior to initiation had identified homozygous *28/*28 status, conferring heightened risk for irinotecan-related grade 3-4 diarrhea and neutropenia — a risk that materialized in cycle 2 with Grade 3 diarrhea requiring a 2-day inpatient hospitalization for IV hydration and electrolyte repletion, and Grade 3 neutropenia (ANC nadir 0.6 x10^9/L). Despite dose reductions to both nal-IRI and 5-FU, restaging CT after 4 cycles of second-line therapy demonstrated clear disease progression: the segment VI hepatic lesion grew from 9 mm to 2.1 cm, the segment VII lesion from 7 mm to 1.6 cm, and two new lesions appeared in segments IV (1.2 cm) and VIII (0.8 cm). CA 19-9 rose sharply to 312 U/mL. ECOG performance status declined to 3.

At the prior clinic visit, following multidisciplinary review and detailed goals-of-care discussion with the patient, her husband, and her daughter, the decision was made jointly to discontinue active cytotoxic chemotherapy and transition to hospice and best supportive care. The patient demonstrated clear understanding of her diagnosis and prognosis, expressed a strong desire to prioritize quality of life and comfort, and wished to remain at home with her family. She declined further chemotherapy or clinical trial enrollment given her markedly diminished functional status.

Today she presents for formal hospice enrollment visit. She reports significant abdominal pain (8/10 at worst, 5-6/10 with current analgesic regimen), worsening fatigue, mild confusion at times (attributed to opioid effect and hepatic encephalopathy), early satiety, nausea, and progressive abdominal distension from ascites. She is spending greater than 50% of her waking hours in bed or in a recliner. She has not been able to tolerate more than small sips of liquids and soft foods for the past week.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Significant fatigue and generalized weakness. Anorexia and profound weight loss — cumulative weight loss from time of initial diagnosis is estimated at greater than 25 lbs. Low-grade intermittent fevers (up to 37.9°C) are reported.
Pain: Constant mid-abdominal and right upper quadrant pain, 5-8/10 in severity, partially responsive to current opioid regimen. Some radiation to the back reported.
Gastrointestinal: Nausea present, partially managed. No vomiting today. Marked abdominal fullness and distension from ascites. Decreased appetite; tolerating only small amounts of soft food and liquids. No hematemesis or melena. Last bowel movement 2 days ago — stools soft, no frank blood.
Neurological: Intermittent mild confusion and somnolence, likely multifactorial (hepatic dysfunction, opioids). No focal neurological deficits. No headache.
Respiratory: Mild dyspnea on minimal exertion, attributable in part to ascites and deconditioning. No pleuritic chest pain, no cough.
Genitourinary: Decreased urinary output noted over the past few days. No dysuria.
Musculoskeletal: Generalized weakness. Residual peripheral neuropathy (grade 1, bilateral hands and feet) from prior oxaliplatin — stable, not a major symptomatic complaint at this time.
Psychiatric: Mood is somber but patient appears at peace with her decisions. She denies active depression or suicidal ideation. She expresses gratitude for her time with family and a desire for a peaceful, comfortable death at home.
Endocrine: Post-pancreatectomy diabetes managed with insulin; glycemic control is less strictly monitored given transition to comfort-focused care.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
1. Metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (Stage IV, M1 liver) — as above
2. Borderline resectable PDAC with biliary obstruction, now status post neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX x 8 cycles, long-course chemoradiation (IMRT 50.4 Gy + concurrent capecitabine), pylorus-preserving Whipple procedure (R0, ypT2 ypN2), adjuvant GemCap (3 cycles), and second-line nal-IRI/5-FU/LV (4 cycles)
3. Grade B postoperative pancreatic fistula — resolved with conservative management
4. Grade B delayed gastric emptying — resolved postoperatively
5. Post-pancreatectomy diabetes mellitus (new insulin-dependent, following Whipple procedure)
6. Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency on Creon (pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy)
7. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — antedating the pancreatic cancer diagnosis; previously on metformin, now on insulin
8. Hepatic metastases with early hepatic dysfunction / encephalopathy
9. Malignant ascites — confirmed on imaging
10. Peripheral sensory neuropathy (grade 1) from prior oxaliplatin — residual
11. Hand-foot syndrome from capecitabine — resolved after capecitabine discontinuation

---

SOCIAL HISTORY
Ms. Choi is a 60-year-old high school librarian who is now on medical leave. She is a lifelong non-smoker. No significant alcohol or illicit substance use. She is married and lives at home with her husband. Her adult daughter lives locally and has been heavily involved in her care. Strong family support system. The patient has expressed clear wishes to die at home; her husband and daughter are fully committed to supporting her through the dying process. Advance directives have been completed: DNR/DNI (Do Not Resuscitate / Do Not Intubate) order in place; POLST form signed. She has a designated healthcare power of attorney (her husband). She has been a practicing Christian and reports that her faith is a source of comfort.

---

FAMILY HISTORY
No family history of pancreatic cancer. Germline testing was offered and declined by the patient at the time of diagnosis (CDKN2A deletion identified on somatic profiling warranted discussion of germline testing, but patient declined). Mother had type 2 diabetes mellitus. Father had coronary artery disease.

---

ALLERGIES
No known drug allergies.

---

MEDICATIONS (AT TIME OF THIS VISIT)
1. Hydromorphone (Dilaudid) oral solution 2 mg/mL — patient-controlled dosing at home via PCA-equivalent oral regimen; 2 mg q4h scheduled with 1 mg q2h breakthrough PRN for pain
2. Haloperidol 0.5 mg oral q8h PRN for nausea and terminal restlessness
3. Lorazepam 0.5 mg sublingual q6h PRN for anxiety/dyspnea
4. Dexamethasone 2 mg orally twice daily (for appetite stimulation, anti-emesis, and sense of well-being — to be tapered as goals shift further toward comfort)
5. Ondansetron 4 mg sublingual q8h PRN for nausea
6. Senna/docusate — bowel regimen to prevent opioid-induced constipation
7. Creon 72,000 lipase units with meals, 36,000 units with snacks — continued for comfort (reducing bloating and steatorrhea)
8. Insulin glargine (Lantus) 10 units subcutaneous at bedtime — dose intentionally reduced from prior 18 units given reduced oral intake and transition to comfort care; fingerstick glucose monitoring discontinued
9. Metformin — previously discontinued at time of chemotherapy initiation; not restarted

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Ms. Choi is a cachectic woman appearing her stated age. She is visibly fatigued but resting comfortably in the exam chair, accompanied by her husband and daughter. She is alert and oriented x3, though slightly slow in responses. She is cooperative and able to communicate clearly when engaged.

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 98/62 mmHg (hypotension, new), heart rate 102 bpm (mild tachycardia), respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 96% on room air, weight 46.2 kg (down from 51 kg at prior visit approximately 4 weeks ago, and down from approximately 72 kg at initial presentation). Temperature 37.4°C.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Mild scleral icterus present (consistent with hepatic dysfunction). Mucous membranes dry. Temporal wasting appreciated. No oral lesions.

Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Peripheral pulses weak but palpable bilaterally.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze, crackles, or rhonchi. Respiratory effort comfortable at rest.

Abdomen: Markedly distended abdomen with shifting dullness and a fluid wave consistent with moderate-to-large volume ascites. Diffuse tenderness to palpation, worse in the right upper quadrant and epigastric region. The liver edge is palpable approximately 6 cm below the right costal margin, consistent with hepatomegaly from metastatic disease. No splenomegaly appreciated. Bowel sounds hypoactive.

Extremities: Bilateral lower extremity pitting edema to the mid-calf (2+), new since last visit. Mild muscle wasting in both thighs. Residual mild paresthesias in bilateral hands and feet (grade 1 neuropathy, not a major concern).

Skin: Sallow complexion. No jaundice of skin appreciated beyond mild icterus. No stigmata of bleeding. No rashes.

Neurological: Alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Mild psychomotor slowing noted. No asterixis appreciated on brief exam. No focal deficits. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Grip strength mildly reduced bilaterally.

Psychological/Affect: Affect is calm and accepting. She reports feeling at peace and states her primary goal is to be comfortable and at home with her family. Appropriate affect to the circumstances.

---

LAB RESULTS (MOST RECENT, FROM PRIOR VISIT APPROXIMATELY 10 DAYS AGO)

CA 19-9: 312 U/mL (markedly elevated, rising trend; previously 58 U/mL at second-line initiation)
Albumin: 2.4 g/dL (low, consistent with malnutrition and hepatic dysfunction)
Total bilirubin: 2.1 mg/dL (elevated, reflecting hepatic metastatic burden and/or early biliary compromise)
Direct bilirubin: 1.5 mg/dL
AST: 87 U/L (elevated, 2x ULN)
ALT: 64 U/L (elevated)
Alkaline phosphatase: 312 U/L (elevated, consistent with hepatic metastases)
Creatinine: 1.4 mg/dL (increased from baseline of 0.7 mg/dL, concerning for hepatorenal physiology or pre-renal azotemia in setting of ascites and decreased oral intake)
BUN: 38 mg/dL
Sodium: 131 mEq/L (hyponatremia, likely dilutional in setting of ascites and hepatic failure)
Potassium: 3.9 mEq/L
Hemoglobin: 8.9 g/dL (anemia, consistent with chronic disease/malnutrition)
WBC: 6.8 x10^9/L
Platelets: 142 x10^9/L (slightly low, likely from portal hypertension and hepatic involvement)
INR: 1.6 (mildly prolonged, consistent with hepatic synthetic dysfunction)

Lab trends overall are consistent with progressive hepatic failure from metastatic disease.

---

IMAGING RESULTS (MOST RECENT)

Most recent restaging CT (approximately 3 weeks prior): Progressive hepatic metastases — the segment VI lesion measured 2.1 cm (up from 9 mm), the segment VII lesion measured 1.6 cm (up from 7 mm), with two new lesions in segments IV (1.2 cm) and VIII (0.8 cm). No locoregional recurrence in the Whipple bed. No peritoneal implants identified on CT, though clinical ascites suggests peritoneal involvement cannot be fully excluded. No pulmonary or osseous metastases identified at that time. No repeat imaging is planned at this visit given the transition to comfort-focused care; further cross-sectional imaging would not change management and would be contrary to the patient's goals.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Choi is a 60-year-old woman with metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, now with widely progressive hepatic metastases after having received three lines of therapy (neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX, adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine, and second-line nal-IRI/5-FU/LV). She is approximately 18 months from her initial diagnosis. Her performance status is ECOG 3, she has progressive hepatic failure (rising bilirubin, coagulopathy, hyponatremia, hypoalbuminemia, hepatomegaly, ascites), worsening malnutrition with cachexia, and declines further active anticancer therapy. Goals of care are comfort-focused; DNR/DNI is in place; she wishes to die at home.

1. FORMAL HOSPICE ENROLLMENT:
   - The patient has been formally enrolled in a home hospice program today. A comprehensive intake evaluation has been completed in coordination with the home hospice interdisciplinary team (attending hospice physician, registered nurse case manager, medical social worker, chaplain, and home health aide services).
   - Hospice benefit covers all comfort-related medications, equipment (hospital bed, bedside commode, wheelchair), nursing visits, and 24-hour on-call nurse support.
   - Hospice team will coordinate with our clinic to assume primary symptom management. Our team will remain available for phone consultation.

2. PAIN MANAGEMENT:
   - Current scheduled hydromorphone 2 mg q4h orally with 1 mg breakthrough q2h PRN. Given worsening pain (now 5-8/10) and likely increasing pain burden as hepatic disease progresses, scheduled dose is being increased to 3 mg q4h orally, with 1.5 mg breakthrough q2h PRN.
   - If oral route becomes unreliable (due to nausea, dysphagia, or decreased level of consciousness), the hospice nurse is authorized to transition to subcutaneous hydromorphone infusion per a standing order provided.
   - Goals: pain control to 3/10 or less, maintaining alertness to the degree compatible with this goal.

3. NAUSEA:
   - Continuing haloperidol 0.5 mg q8h PRN; may be converted to scheduled dosing if nausea worsens.
   - Ondansetron 4 mg sublingual q8h PRN continued.
   - Dexamethasone 2 mg twice daily is continued for its antiemetic and appetite-stimulating benefit; will be tapered or converted to subcutaneous if swallowing becomes compromised.
   - If refractory nausea develops, hospice team has standing order for prochlorperazine suppository.

4. MALIGNANT ASCITES:
   - A therapeutic paracentesis was discussed with the patient and family as an option for symptomatic relief of abdominal distension and dyspnea. The patient deferred paracentesis today but expressed openness to it if discomfort from ascites becomes intolerable. Hospice nurse can facilitate a home paracentesis or arrange outpatient procedure; the patient and family understand how to request this.
   - No diuretics initiated given hypotension, hyponatremia, and renal function decline; diuresis would carry unacceptable risk of further hemodynamic compromise in this setting.

5. HEPATIC ENCEPHALOPATHY:
   - Mild at present. Lactulose titrated to 2-3 soft stools per day was considered but given the patient's already reduced oral intake and goal of minimizing pill burden, this will only be initiated if encephalopathy worsens significantly and if consistent with her comfort goals.
   - Family educated on signs of worsening encephalopathy (confusion, agitation, somnolence) and what to expect as hepatic failure progresses.

6. DYSPNEA:
   - Lorazepam 0.5 mg sublingual q6h PRN continues for dyspnea and anxiety.
   - If dyspnea worsens, low-dose oral or subcutaneous morphine may be added as a dyspnea-specific agent even in the absence of pain, per hospice standing order. The hospice nurse and physician have been briefed on this plan.
   - Home oxygen available through hospice for symptomatic relief of dyspnea if SpO2 declines or patient requests it.

7. NUTRITION AND HYDRATION:
   - Artificial nutrition and IV hydration are not being pursued, consistent with the patient's wishes and given the terminal trajectory. Eating and drinking as tolerated for pleasure and comfort are encouraged. Small, frequent, preferred foods are recommended.
   - Creon is continued for GI comfort (reduction of bloating, gas, and fat malabsorption symptoms).
   - Mouth care (ice chips, moistened swabs) provided at bedside for dry mouth.

8. INSULIN AND DIABETES MANAGEMENT:
   - Insulin glargine has been reduced to 10 units nightly. Fingerstick glucose monitoring discontinued — intensive glycemic control is not appropriate given the patient's comfort-focused goals. Insulin will be adjusted or discontinued by the hospice team as oral intake declines further and hypoglycemia risk increases.

9. PSYCHOSOCIAL AND SPIRITUAL SUPPORT:
   - Social worker has been formally engaged with the family to provide bereavement pre-counseling and assist with practical end-of-life planning (funeral arrangements, financial affairs).
   - Chaplaincy available through the hospice for spiritual support; the patient has indicated her Christian faith community pastor is already visiting regularly and providing spiritual care.
   - The patient's husband and daughter have been counseled regarding caregiver burden, anticipatory grief, and self-care. Caregiver respite services are available through the hospice as needed.
   - The family has been educated on the expected signs and symptoms of the actively dying process (Cheyne-Stokes breathing, mottling, decreased consciousness, decreased urination) and reassured that the hospice team will provide continuous guidance.

10. CODE STATUS AND ADVANCE DIRECTIVES:
    - DNR/DNI confirmed. POLST signed. Healthcare power of attorney (husband) documented. All documents have been transmitted to the hospice agency.
    - Patient does not wish to return to the hospital or emergency room; preferred site of death is home. This has been communicated clearly to the hospice team.

11. FOLLOW-UP:
    - No further oncology follow-up visits are scheduled. Our team remains available by phone for consultation with the hospice team or family as needed.
    - Outpatient oncology record has been updated to reflect transition to hospice; further lab work or imaging is not planned.

A deeply sincere conversation was held with Ms. Choi, her husband, and her daughter today. The patient was thanked for her trust in our care team over the course of her illness. She expressed gratitude, composure, and clarity about her wishes. Her fortitude and dignity throughout an extraordinarily difficult 18-month illness were acknowledged. The entire care team wishes her peace and comfort in her remaining time at home with her loved ones.

Attending Oncologist signature on file.
Palliative Medicine co-signature on file.
Time spent: 75 minutes (greater than 50% of the visit spent in counseling and care coordination).
